• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

What Predicts Crime? A Socioeconomic Analysis of US States

Correlation, Multiple Regression & 10-Year Longitudinal Analysis

[MD Noornabi] | Mayerfeld Data Analytics Certificate

March 2026

Background & Central Question

This analysis uses a dataset of 47 US states measured at two timepoints — Year 0 and Year 10 — covering crime rates and 13 socioeconomic variables including wages, education, police expenditure, unemployment, and demographics.

Three questions drive the analysis:

  1. Which socioeconomic variables significantly predict crime rates?
  2. Are the relationships what we’d intuitively expect — or are there paradoxes that require deeper interpretation?
  3. How stable are crime rates over a decade — and do the same predictors hold at both timepoints?

Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggcorrplot)
library(gridExtra)
library(moments)
library(scales)

mytheme <- theme_minimal(base_size=13) +
  theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",size=14,color="#1F4E79"),
        plot.subtitle=element_text(color="grey40",size=11),
        plot.caption=element_text(color="grey55",size=9,face="italic"),
        panel.grid.minor=element_blank())

cohens_d <- function(x,y) (mean(x)-mean(y)) / sqrt((sd(x)^2+sd(y)^2)/2)
crime <- read.csv("data/Crime.csv")
crime$Southern       <- factor(crime$Southern,       levels=c(0,1), labels=c("Non-Southern","Southern"))
crime$MoreMales      <- factor(crime$MoreMales,       levels=c(0,1), labels=c("No","Yes"))
crime$HighYouthUnemploy <- factor(crime$HighYouthUnemploy, levels=c(0,1), labels=c("Low","High"))
crime$LogExpenditure <- log(crime$ExpenditureYear0)
crime$CrimeChange    <- crime$CrimeRate10 - crime$CrimeRate
cat("n =", nrow(crime), "states  |  Variables:", ncol(crime))
## n = 47 states  |  Variables: 29

1. Exploratory Data Analysis

Crime Rate Overview

data.frame(
  Timepoint = c("Year 0","Year 10"),
  Mean      = round(c(mean(crime$CrimeRate),mean(crime$CrimeRate10)),2),
  Median    = round(c(median(crime$CrimeRate),median(crime$CrimeRate10)),2),
  SD        = round(c(sd(crime$CrimeRate),sd(crime$CrimeRate10)),2),
  Min       = c(min(crime$CrimeRate),min(crime$CrimeRate10)),
  Max       = c(max(crime$CrimeRate),max(crime$CrimeRate10))
)
cr_long <- data.frame(
  CrimeRate = c(crime$CrimeRate,crime$CrimeRate10),
  Timepoint = rep(c("Year 0","Year 10"),each=nrow(crime))
)
ggplot(cr_long, aes(CrimeRate,fill=Timepoint,color=Timepoint)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y=after_stat(density)),bins=15,alpha=0.45,position="identity") +
  geom_density(linewidth=1.1,alpha=0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=mean(crime$CrimeRate),  linetype="dashed",color="#2980b9",linewidth=0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=mean(crime$CrimeRate10),linetype="dashed",color="#e67e22",linewidth=0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("Year 0"="#2980b9","Year 10"="#e67e22")) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("Year 0"="#2980b9","Year 10"="#e67e22")) +
  labs(title="Crime Rate Distribution — Year 0 vs Year 10",
       subtitle="Dashed lines = group means  ·  n=47 states",
       x="Crime Rate (offences per million)",y="Density") + mytheme

Figure 1: Crime rate distributions are broadly similar across both timepoints, with slightly greater spread at Year 10.

Handling Skewed Variables

data.frame(
  Variable      = c("ExpenditureYear0","Log(ExpenditureYear0)","YouthUnemployment","Log(YouthUnemployment)"),
  Skewness      = round(c(skewness(crime$ExpenditureYear0),
                           skewness(log(crime$ExpenditureYear0)),
                           skewness(crime$YouthUnemployment),
                           skewness(log(crime$YouthUnemployment))),4),
  Action        = c("Log-transformed","✓ Used in regression","Noted","Not needed — not in final model")
)
exp_df <- data.frame(
  Value = c(crime$ExpenditureYear0,log(crime$ExpenditureYear0)),
  Type  = rep(c("Original (skewness = 0.92)","Log-transformed (skewness = 0.28)"),each=nrow(crime))
)
ggplot(exp_df, aes(Value,fill=Type)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=15,alpha=0.85,color="white") +
  facet_wrap(~Type,scales="free_x") +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("#e74c3c","#27ae60")) +
  labs(title="Police Expenditure: Correcting Positive Skew",
       x="Value",y="Count") +
  mytheme + theme(legend.position="none")

Figure 2: Log transformation substantially reduces right skew in police expenditure, making it more appropriate for regression.

2. Correlation Analysis: What Predicts Crime?

cont_vars <- c("Youth","Education","ExpenditureYear0","LabourForce","Males",
               "StateSize","YouthUnemployment","MatureUnemployment","Wage","BelowWage")
map_dfr(cont_vars, function(v) {
  ct <- cor.test(crime[[v]], crime$CrimeRate)
  tibble(Variable=v, r=round(ct$estimate,4), p=round(ct$p.value,4),
         Significant=ifelse(ct$p.value<0.05,"Yes **","No"))
}) %>% arrange(desc(abs(r)))
all_cont <- crime %>% select(CrimeRate,Youth,Education,ExpenditureYear0,LabourForce,
                               Males,StateSize,YouthUnemployment,MatureUnemployment,Wage,BelowWage)
ggcorrplot(cor(all_cont), hc.order=TRUE, type="lower", lab=TRUE, lab_size=3,
           colors=c("#c0392b","white","#1a7abf"), outline.color="white",
           title="Correlation Matrix — All Continuous Variables") +
  mytheme + theme(axis.text.x=element_text(angle=45,hjust=1))

Figure 3: Correlation heatmap with hierarchical clustering. Police expenditure and wage are the strongest crime correlates. Note the very strong negative correlation between Wage and BelowWage (r = -0.88). 

top3 <- crime %>%
  select(CrimeRate,ExpenditureYear0,Wage,StateSize,Southern) %>%
  pivot_longer(c(ExpenditureYear0,Wage,StateSize),names_to="Predictor",values_to="Value") %>%
  mutate(Predictor=recode(Predictor,
    "ExpenditureYear0"="Police Expenditure (r=+0.65**)",
    "Wage"="Median Wage (r=+0.42**)",
    "StateSize"="State Size (r=+0.31*)"))
ggplot(top3, aes(Value,CrimeRate,color=Southern)) +
  geom_point(size=2.8,alpha=0.85) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm",se=TRUE,aes(group=1),color="#2c3e50",fill="grey70",alpha=0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~Predictor,scales="free_x") +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("Non-Southern"="#2980b9","Southern"="#e74c3c")) +
  labs(title="Top Three Predictors of Crime Rate",
       subtitle="All three are positive — and all three require careful interpretation beyond face value",
       x=NULL,y="Crime Rate",color="Region") + mytheme

Figure 4: The three significant continuous predictors of crime rate. All show positive relationships — each is counterintuitive and requires interpretation.

3. The Three Paradoxes

Paradox 1 — Police Expenditure: Spending Cause Crime or Respond to It?

The strongest predictor of crime is police expenditure (r = +0.65). At first glance this seems absurd — more police spending leads to more crime? The obvious alternative is reverse causation: high-crime states spend more on policing because of high crime.

But we can test this. If reverse causation fully explains the relationship, then Year 0 spending should not predict Year 10 crime — because the spending is responding to current crime, not causing future crime.

data.frame(
  Test = c("Exp(Year 0) vs Crime(Year 0)","Exp(Year 0) vs Crime(Year 10)"),
  r    = round(c(cor(crime$ExpenditureYear0,crime$CrimeRate),
                  cor(crime$ExpenditureYear0,crime$CrimeRate10)),4),
  p    = round(c(cor.test(crime$ExpenditureYear0,crime$CrimeRate)$p.value,
                  cor.test(crime$ExpenditureYear0,crime$CrimeRate10)$p.value),4)
)
r_exp10 <- round(cor(crime$ExpenditureYear0,crime$CrimeRate10),3)
ggplot(crime, aes(ExpenditureYear0,CrimeRate10,color=Southern)) +
  geom_point(size=3,alpha=0.85) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm",se=TRUE,aes(group=1),color="#2c3e50",fill="grey70",alpha=0.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("Non-Southern"="#2980b9","Southern"="#e74c3c")) +
  labs(title="The Expenditure Paradox: Year 0 Spending → Year 10 Crime",
       subtitle=paste0("r = ",r_exp10,", p < 0.001  ·  Forward-looking prediction rules out pure reverse causation\n",
                       "Interpretation: high-crime states invest heavily in policing, which becomes structurally embedded"),
       x="Police Expenditure per Capita — Year 0",y="Crime Rate — Year 10",color="Region") + mytheme

Figure 5: Year 0 police expenditure predicts Year 10 crime almost as strongly as Year 0 crime. Pure reverse causation cannot explain this forward-looking relationship.

Interpretation: The relationship is likely bidirectional and structural. High-crime states invest more in policing. That investment becomes embedded in state infrastructure. States with historically high crime maintain high expenditure — and high crime — over time.

Paradox 2 — Wage: Does Prosperity Drive Crime?

Higher wages are associated with more crime (r = +0.42). But the same states with higher wages have dramatically fewer families below the poverty line (r = −0.88 between Wage and BelowWage).

data.frame(
  Relationship = c("Wage vs CrimeRate","BelowWage vs CrimeRate","Wage vs BelowWage"),
  r = round(c(cor(crime$Wage,crime$CrimeRate),
               cor(crime$BelowWage,crime$CrimeRate),
               cor(crime$Wage,crime$BelowWage)),4),
  p = round(c(cor.test(crime$Wage,crime$CrimeRate)$p.value,
               cor.test(crime$BelowWage,crime$CrimeRate)$p.value,
               cor.test(crime$Wage,crime$BelowWage)$p.value),4)
)

Interpretation: Wage is a proxy for urbanisation. High-wage states tend to be more urban. Urban areas have higher crime rates (more density, more opportunity, more reporting) — but they also have greater prosperity and less poverty. Wage is not a direct driver of crime; it’s a confounding variable capturing the urban/rural dimension.

Paradox 3 — Education: Why Doesn’t Education Reduce Crime?

data.frame(
  Test = c("Education(t0) vs Crime(t0)","Education(t10) vs Crime(t10)"),
  r    = round(c(cor(crime$Education,crime$CrimeRate),
                  cor(crime$Education10,crime$CrimeRate10)),4),
  p    = round(c(cor.test(crime$Education,crime$CrimeRate)$p.value,
                  cor.test(crime$Education10,crime$CrimeRate10)$p.value),4),
  Direction = c("Positive (!)","Positive (!)")
)

More education is slightly (non-significantly) associated with more crime at both timepoints — the same urbanisation confound. More educated states tend to be more urban. The direct protective effect of education is masked by this confounding relationship.

4. Group Comparisons

Southern vs Non-Southern States

vars_comp <- c("CrimeRate","Wage","Education","ExpenditureYear0","BelowWage")
map_dfr(vars_comp, function(v) {
  s  <- crime %>% filter(Southern=="Southern") %>% pull(v)
  ns <- crime %>% filter(Southern=="Non-Southern") %>% pull(v)
  tt <- t.test(s, ns, var.equal=FALSE)
  tibble(Variable=v,
         `South Mean`=round(mean(s),2),
         `NonSouth Mean`=round(mean(ns),2),
         `p-value`=round(tt$p.value,4),
         `Cohen's d`=round(abs(cohens_d(s,ns)),3),
         Significant=ifelse(tt$p.value<0.05,"Yes **","No"))
})
south_long <- crime %>%
  select(Southern,CrimeRate,Wage,Education,ExpenditureYear0,BelowWage) %>%
  pivot_longer(-Southern,names_to="Variable",values_to="Value") %>%
  mutate(Variable=recode(Variable,
    "CrimeRate"="Crime Rate\n(p=0.691)",
    "Wage"="Median Wage\n(p<0.001 **)",
    "Education"="Education Years\n(p=0.001 **)",
    "ExpenditureYear0"="Police Expenditure\n(p=0.005 **)",
    "BelowWage"="Below Poverty\n(p<0.001 **)"))
ggplot(south_long, aes(Southern,Value,fill=Southern)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha=0.75,width=0.5) +
  geom_jitter(width=0.12,alpha=0.5,size=1.8,color="grey30") +
  facet_wrap(~Variable,scales="free_y",nrow=1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("Non-Southern"="#2980b9","Southern"="#e74c3c")) +
  labs(title="Southern States: Similar Crime Rates, Very Different Conditions",
       subtitle="Wages, education, spending, and poverty all differ significantly — crime rate does not",
       x=NULL,y=NULL) +
  mytheme + theme(legend.position="none",axis.text.x=element_text(angle=30,hjust=1))

Figure 6: Southern states show similar crime rates to non-Southern states but dramatically worse socioeconomic conditions across every measured variable.

Key insight: Southern states have lower wages, less education, lower police expenditure, and more families in poverty — yet their crime rates are not significantly higher. This suggests that crime rate is determined by a complex combination of factors that don’t reduce to simple economic disadvantage, and that the structural embedding of crime patterns (see Section 6) may be as important as current conditions.

High vs Low Youth Unemployment

high <- crime %>% filter(HighYouthUnemploy=="High")
low  <- crime %>% filter(HighYouthUnemploy=="Low")
tt   <- t.test(high$CrimeRate, low$CrimeRate, var.equal=FALSE)
cat(sprintf("High (n=%d): mean=%.1f  |  Low (n=%d): mean=%.1f\nt=%.3f  p=%.3f  d=%.3f",
            nrow(high), mean(high$CrimeRate), nrow(low), mean(low$CrimeRate),
            tt$statistic, tt$p.value, abs(cohens_d(high$CrimeRate, low$CrimeRate))))
## High (n=15): mean=90.9  |  Low (n=32): mean=108.4
## t=-1.948  p=0.062  d=0.618

Borderline result (p = 0.062) with a medium effect size (d ≈ 0.60). This is likely a Type II error — the sample of 47 states is insufficient to reliably detect a moderate group difference. With a larger dataset, this would likely reach significance.

5. Multiple Linear Regression

Building the Model

model <- lm(CrimeRate ~ LogExpenditure + Wage + StateSize + BelowWage, data=crime)
summary(model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = CrimeRate ~ LogExpenditure + Wage + StateSize + 
##     BelowWage, data = crime)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -56.064 -11.582   2.533  12.249  31.839 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)    -441.53199   75.27136  -5.866 6.19e-07 ***
## LogExpenditure   86.35176   16.47156   5.242 4.83e-06 ***
## Wage              0.11043    0.08277   1.334  0.18932    
## StateSize        -0.14614    0.08694  -1.681  0.10022    
## BelowWage         0.58163    0.15572   3.735  0.00056 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 18.5 on 42 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6257, Adjusted R-squared:   0.59 
## F-statistic: 17.55 on 4 and 42 DF,  p-value: 1.546e-08
sw <- shapiro.test(residuals(model))
cat(sprintf("Shapiro-Wilk on residuals: W=%.4f  p=%.4f  [%s]",
            sw$statistic, sw$p.value, ifelse(sw$p.value>0.05,"Normal ✓","Not Normal ✗")))
## Shapiro-Wilk on residuals: W=0.9636  p=0.1488  [Normal ✓]
crime$predicted <- fitted(model)
ggplot(crime, aes(predicted,CrimeRate,color=Southern)) +
  geom_point(size=3,alpha=0.85) +
  geom_abline(slope=1,intercept=0,linetype="dashed",color="grey40",linewidth=1) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm",se=FALSE,aes(group=1),color="#2c3e50",linewidth=0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("Non-Southern"="#2980b9","Southern"="#e74c3c")) +
  labs(title="Multiple Regression: Predicted vs Actual Crime Rate",
       subtitle=paste0("R² = ",round(summary(model)$r.squared,3),
                       "  |  Adjusted R² = ",round(summary(model)$adj.r.squared,3)),
       x="Predicted",y="Actual",color="Region") + mytheme

Figure 7: The model predicts crime rate well — points cluster tightly around the diagonal. R² = 0.629 means the four predictors together explain 63% of variance in crime rate. 

crime$residuals <- residuals(model)
ggplot(crime, aes(predicted,residuals)) +
  geom_point(color="#2980b9",size=3,alpha=0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0,linetype="dashed",color="grey40",linewidth=1) +
  geom_smooth(se=FALSE,color="#e74c3c",linewidth=0.9) +
  labs(title="Residual Plot — Homoscedasticity Check",
       x="Fitted Values",y="Residuals") + mytheme

Figure 8: Residuals show roughly even spread — homoscedasticity is approximately satisfied. 

std_model <- lm(scale(CrimeRate) ~ scale(LogExpenditure)+scale(Wage)+
                  scale(StateSize)+scale(BelowWage), data=crime)
coef_df <- data.frame(
  Predictor = c("Log(Police Expenditure)","Median Wage","State Size","Below-Wage Families"),
  Beta      = coef(std_model)[-1],
  SE        = summary(std_model)$coefficients[-1,2],
  p_val     = summary(std_model)$coefficients[-1,4]
) %>% mutate(
  Significant=ifelse(p_val<0.05,"Significant (p<0.05)","Not significant")
) %>% arrange(abs(Beta))
coef_df$Predictor <- factor(coef_df$Predictor,levels=coef_df$Predictor)

ggplot(coef_df, aes(Beta,Predictor,color=Significant)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=0,linetype="dashed",color="grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin=Beta-1.96*SE,xmax=Beta+1.96*SE),height=0.2,linewidth=1.1) +
  geom_point(size=5) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("Significant (p<0.05)"="#1a7abf","Not significant"="#e74c3c")) +
  labs(title="Standardised Regression Coefficients",
       subtitle="95% CI error bars  ·  All predictors on same scale",
       x="Standardised β",y=NULL,color=NULL) + mytheme

Figure 9: Standardised coefficients allow direct comparison of predictor importance. Log(Expenditure) and Wage are the dominant predictors.

Model Summary

Metric Value
R² 0.626
Adjusted R² 0.59
Residuals normal (Shapiro-Wilk) Yes ✓
Dominant predictor Log(Police Expenditure)

Four predictors explain 62.6% of variance in state crime rates. This is a strong result for observational social science data. The dominant predictor — log(police expenditure) — reflects the structural embedding discussed in Paradox 1.

6. Longitudinal Analysis: 10-Year Persistence

Did Crime Rates Change Overall?

tt_long <- t.test(crime$CrimeRate, crime$CrimeRate10, paired=TRUE)
cat(sprintf("Paired t-test: t=%.3f  p=%.3f\nMean change: %.2f  SD: %.2f\nIncreased: %d/47  Decreased: %d/47",
            tt_long$statistic, tt_long$p.value,
            mean(crime$CrimeChange), sd(crime$CrimeChange),
            sum(crime$CrimeChange>0), sum(crime$CrimeChange<0)))
## Paired t-test: t=0.471  p=0.640
## Mean change: -0.74  SD: 10.75
## Increased: 27/47  Decreased: 20/47

No significant overall change (p = 0.640) — but 27 states increased and 20 decreased, with the average change near zero.

The Persistence Finding

r_persist <- cor.test(crime$CrimeRate, crime$CrimeRate10)
cat(sprintf("r = %.4f  p < 0.001", r_persist$estimate))
## r = 0.9966  p < 0.001
ggplot(crime, aes(CrimeRate,CrimeRate10,color=Southern)) +
  geom_point(size=3,alpha=0.85) +
  geom_abline(slope=1,intercept=0,linetype="dashed",color="grey50",linewidth=1) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm",se=TRUE,aes(group=1),color="#2c3e50",fill="grey70",alpha=0.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("Non-Southern"="#2980b9","Southern"="#e74c3c")) +
  annotate("text",x=55,y=170,
           label=paste0("r = ",round(r_persist$estimate,3),"\np < 0.001"),
           size=4.5,color="#1F4E79",fontface="bold",hjust=0) +
  labs(title=paste0("Crime Rate Persistence Across 10 Years  (r = ",
                    round(r_persist$estimate,3),")"),
       subtitle="States maintain their relative crime ranking almost perfectly over a decade",
       x="Crime Rate — Year 0",y="Crime Rate — Year 10",
       caption="Above diagonal = increased; below = decreased",
       color="Region") + mytheme

Figure 10: Near-perfect correlation between Year 0 and Year 10 crime rates. A state’s crime rank is essentially locked in — structural change is minimal over a decade. 

crime %>%
  mutate(State=row_number(),
         Direction=ifelse(CrimeChange>0,"Increased","Decreased")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x=reorder(State,CrimeChange),y=CrimeChange,fill=Direction)) +
  geom_col(alpha=0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0,color="grey30",linewidth=0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=mean(crime$CrimeChange),linetype="dashed",color="#2c3e50",linewidth=1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("Increased"="#e74c3c","Decreased"="#27ae60")) +
  labs(title="Crime Rate Change per State Over 10 Years",
       subtitle=paste0("27 increased, 20 decreased  |  Mean change = ",
                       round(mean(crime$CrimeChange),2)," (p = 0.640, not significant)"),
       x="States (ordered by change)",y="Change in Crime Rate",fill="Direction") +
  mytheme + theme(axis.text.x=element_blank(),axis.ticks.x=element_blank())

Figure 11: Individual state changes are modest and roughly balanced — no systemic direction of change, consistent with the non-significant paired t-test.

7. Summary of Findings

Finding Result Interpretation
Strongest predictor Police expenditure (r=+0.65) Structural bidirectional relationship — not simple causation
Wage paradox r=+0.42 with crime Urbanisation confound — wealth and crime co-occur in urban states
Education r=+0.16, not significant Same urbanisation confound masks protective effect
Southern states Crime p=0.691 (ns) Similar crime, but lower wages/education/spending, more poverty
Youth unemployment p=0.062 (borderline) Medium effect — likely Type II error with n=47
Regression R² 0.629 Four predictors explain 63% of crime rate variance
10-year persistence r=0.997 Crime rate is a deeply structural state characteristic
Overall change p=0.640 (ns) No significant change in mean crime rate over decade

Core insight: Crime rates across US states reflect deep structural characteristics that persist almost unchanged over a decade. The predictors that matter — expenditure, wage (as urbanisation proxy), state size — are themselves structural, slow-moving variables. Short-term policy interventions are unlikely to shift crime rates materially; the patterns are embedded in the social and economic fabric of states.

Analysis in R — tidyverse, ggplot2, ggcorrplot. Mayerfeld Data Analytics Certificate — Week 3.
